validator_independence.summary: OP-003-bf16 validator independently benchmarked the BF16 SSM kernel using a 120 MB L2 flush (2.5x L2 size), producing baseline_cold=175.82us vs proposal's nsys-calibrated 123.1us (1.43x divergence). The validator's methodology was MORE conservative -- production cold sits between the two. Both confirm the optimization works; 2.08x passes the 2.0x kill criterion regardless.

citation.claim: Validator independently benchmarked kernel with conservative L2 flush methodology, diverging 1.43x from proposal baseline but still passing kill criterion
citation.quoted_content:
| 32 | 52.70 | 26.08 | 175.82 | 84.67 | **2.08x** |

**Note on baseline_cold divergence**: Validator's baseline_cold=175.82us vs nsys production=122.3us. The validator's flush evicts ALL tensors including 2MB read-only params (A, D, dt_bias) that are L2-warm in production.

validator_independence.summary: OP-003-bf16 validator independently tested correctness across 512 decode steps with random per-step inputs, verifying that BF16 state error is stable and does not accumulate (SSM recurrence is strongly contractive). Cosine sim 0.99989-0.99991 across BS=1,8,32.

citation.claim: Validator independently verified 512-step accuracy stability and non-accumulation of BF16 error
citation.quoted_content:
| 1 | 0.99989378 | 1.825% | None | PASS |
| 8 | 0.99990577 | 1.561% | None | PASS |
| 32 | 0.99990702 | 1.539% | None | PASS |

- State error is stable (does not accumulate) -- SSM recurrence is strongly contractive

validator_independence.summary: OP-001-ext validator independently tested both Marlin W1 fused (max_abs 7.81e-3 vs unquantized reference) and Triton relu2 fused (bit-identical, 0.0 max diff) components, plus CUDA graph capture/replay verification.

citation.claim: Validator independently confirmed Marlin max_abs 7.81e-3 and Triton bit-identical correctness across all batch sizes
citation.quoted_content:
**Group A -- Marlin W1 fused vs unquantized reference (atol=0.1):**
| BS | max_abs | Result |
| 1 | 7.81e-3 | PASS |
...
**Group B -- Triton fused relu2 vs PyTorch reference:**
| Shape | max_abs | Result |
| All shapes, BF16/FP16 | **0.0** (bit-identical) | PASS |

validator_independence.summary: OP-002-v2 validator independently identified the root cause of split-K regression: FP32 workspace writes 6x more bandwidth than BF16 direct output, plus reduced K-loop pipeline efficiency (84% to 55%). Also confirmed StreamK already provides partial work redistribution.

citation.claim: Validator independently determined FP32 workspace + pipeline depth degradation as root cause of 13-17% regression
citation.quoted_content:
### 1. FP32 Workspace Bandwidth Overhead
Split-K writes FP32 partials to workspace before reducing, adding significant write bandwidth:
- out_proj (sk=3): writes 3 x 32 x 2688 x 4 = 1.03 MB FP32 (vs baseline writing 32 x 2688 x 2 = 172 KB BF16)
- This is ~6x more write bandwidth than the baseline

### 2. Reduced K-Loop Pipeline Depth
...Baseline out_proj: K=4096, K_tile=128, 32 K-iterations per CTA (pipeline 84% efficient)

campaign_metrics.termination_reason: 3 rounds, 7 implementations, remaining bottleneck (MoE GEMM 75.9%) at BW ceiling -- proven unoptimizable across 3 rounds and 5 separate FP8/BF16 GEMM attempts
technology_classes: BF16 Mamba SSM state dtype change (FP32 -> BF16, L2 residency regime shift)
Dual relu2 epilogue fusion (Marlin MoE W1 CUDA epilogue + shared expert Triton kernel, 69 kernel launches eliminated/step)
CUTLASS FP8 split-K (proposed, failed: EVT blocks kGemmSplitKParallel, 1.04x)
CUTLASS FP8 EVT-bypass split-K (proposed, failed: 13-17% regression from FP32 workspace overhead)
Triton FP8 split-K GEMM (proposed, eliminated in debate: Triton 14-43% slower than CUTLASS)
Triton BF16 split-K GEMM (proposed, failed: slower than cuBLAS for K>2688)
Fused MoE dispatch (proposed, failed: single-CTA BW 44 GB/s, 13.6x below proposal's 600 GB/s assumption)

anti_patterns.description: Champion-1 projected 5.67% E2E at BS=32 using f=0.148 and 1.62x kernel speedup. The f=0.148 conflated 'Dense GEMM (FP8 CUTLASS)' (14,024 ms including BF16 GEMM) with FP8 CUTLASS-only (7,118 ms, f=0.0753). Combined with fabricated 68.5 us/call profiling match (actual: 34.8 us/call), the original proposal was 2.4x above the achievable ceiling. OP-002-splitk ultimately failed at 1.04x kernel speedup.

citations.claim: Champion-1 used f=0.148 while simultaneously citing 7,118 ms FP8 CUTLASS total (internally inconsistent: 7118/94487=0.0753)
citations.quoted_content:
| Total FP8 CUTLASS GPU time | 7,118 ms / 2560 steps = **2,780 us/step** | bottleneck_analysis.md S2 |
| Per-step share (f) | **0.148** | bottleneck_analysis.md S2 |

citations.claim: Champion-1 projected 5.67% E2E at BS=32 using the inflated f=0.148
citations.quoted_content:
E2E improvement = f x (1 - 1/kernel_speedup)
                = 0.148 x (1 - 1/1.62)
                = 0.148 x 0.383
                = 5.67%

citations.claim: Champion-1 conceded corrected f=0.0753, dropping E2E from 5.7% to 1.8-2.4%
citations.quoted_content:
**Major concession**: The bottleneck analysis labels "Dense GEMM (FP8 CUTLASS)" with f=0.148, but the Top-15 kernel data shows FP8 CUTLASS total is 7,118 ms = **f=0.0753**. The remaining 6,906 ms is BF16 GEMM (lm_head, excluded layers). My proposal targets only the FP8 CUTLASS subset.

**Corrected f = 0.0753**

anti_patterns.description: Champion-1 claimed L2-cold benchmark matched profiling at '68.5 us/call within 1.2%'. The 68.5 us figure does not exist in bottleneck_analysis.md or constraints.md. Actual production per-call average is (5117+2001)ms / (160965+43435) instances = 34.8 us/call -- approximately 2x lower. Champion-3 identified this in critique.

citations.claim: Champion-1 fabricated 68.5 us/call figure not traceable to any source artifact
citations.quoted_content: **Validation**: Weighted average = 69.3 us/call, matching profiling data (68.5 us/call) within 1.2%. L2-cold methodology is sound.

citations.claim: Actual per-call averages are 31.8 us (variant A) and 46.1 us (variant B), weighted ~34.8 us -- not 68.5 us
citations.quoted_content:
**Grounded evidence**:
- Average kernel time: 31.8 us (variant A), 46.1 us (variant B)

citations.claim: Champion-3 identified cold times ~2x inflated and challenged 68.5 us as double-count
citations.quoted_content:
Champion-1 claims "L2-cold matches profiling within 1.2%" (weighted 69.3 vs 68.5 us). But the profiled weighted average per call is: (5117 + 2001) ms / 2560 steps / 80 calls = 34.8 us/call

NOT 68.5 us.

anti_patterns.description: Champion-1 continued projecting 4.7-6.5% E2E using f=0.148 through the entire Phase-A argument, presenting this as 'grounded from profiling', even though the proposal already listed the 7,118 ms total from which f=0.0753 is directly derivable. Error persisted through argument and critique phases before concession in rebuttal.

citations.claim: Champion-1 cited f=0.148 as 'grounded from profiling' and projected 6.5% E2E in Phase A
citations.quoted_content:
f = 0.148 (BS=32, grounded from profiling)
E2E = f x (1-1/speedup) = 0.148 x (1 - 1/1.79) = 0.148 x 0.441 = 6.5%

citations.claim: Champion-1 summary table repeated f=0.148 and 4.7-6.5% as official grounded range
citations.quoted_content:
| f (BS=32) | 0.148 | bottleneck_analysis.md S2 |
...| E2E (BS=32) | **4.7-6.5%** | f x (1-1/speedup) |

anti_patterns.description: Both Round 1 debate selections (OP-002-splitk score 6.80, OP-001-relu2 score 7.70) failed implementation gates, consuming 6 impl-agent slots for ~84 minutes. Zero shipped optimizations from Round 1; all shipped OPs came from Round 2's entirely new debate. Root cause: debate accepted theoretical projections as sufficient feasibility evidence without requiring empirical kernel prototypes.

citations.claim: Both Round 1 candidates failed: OP-002-splitk (EVT blocks, 1.04x) and OP-001-relu2 (0.58% < 1.0% threshold)

citations.claim: Debate scoring gave OP-002-splitk 6/10 feasibility despite zero empirical split-K benchmarks
citations.quoted_content: | Feasibility evidence | 25% | 6/10 | Strong production profiling analysis (variant A 37% BW vs variant B 71% BW). BF16 cuBLAS cross-validation. BUT no empirical CUTLASS split-K benchmark. Theoretical argument sound but unvalidated. |

causality_chains.summary: Discovered StreamK + SplitK interaction (ThreadblockSwizzleStreamK already active in scaled_mm_c2x.cuh line 88) and K=3712 prime factorization constraint. Champion-3 cited verbatim as Weakness 4 in critique. Findings confirmed by OP-002-v2 validation.

citation.claim: Delegate-3a identified StreamK + SplitK interaction and shared_dn prime K factorization
citation.quoted_content: **This is critical:** The swizzle is `ThreadblockSwizzleStreamK`, NOT the standard identity swizzle. This affects split-K behavior... `shared_dn` (K=3712, TileK=128): only batch_count=1 or 29 are exact.

causality_chains.summary: Corrected bottleneck_analysis.md line 342 error: empty MoE experts are already excluded by moe_align_block_size, not reading weights for all experts. Also confirmed shared expert uses ReLUSquaredActivation (relu2). Both findings cited by Champion-2 in proposal, validating the extended relu2 optimization that shipped.

citation.claim: Delegate-2a corrected bottleneck_analysis.md error: empty experts are already excluded, not reading weights
citation.quoted_content:
## CRITICAL FINDING: Empty Experts Are NOT in the Grid

**Empty experts (0 tokens) do NOT get any CTAs launched.** They are excluded during `moe_align_block_size`. The optimization hypothesis was incorrect.

causality_chains.summary: Computed per-shape roofline for all 4 FP8 CUTLASS shapes showing variant A at 37-39% BW util / 2.54-2.67x ceiling vs variant B at 71% / 1.40x. This differentiation was new information not in bottleneck_analysis.md aggregate. Champion-3 cited directly.

citation.claim: Delegate-3a computed per-shape ceilings differentiating variant A (2.54-2.67x) from variant B (1.40x)

delegation_analysis.counterfactual_assessment: Delegates provided moderate positive value. The StreamK interaction (delegate-3a) and empty-expert correction (delegate-2a) would likely not have been surfaced without delegate code inspection. Champion-2's scope extension to shared experts -- which made OP-001-ext pass the kill threshold -- was grounded by delegate-2a's shared_expert_codepath confirmation. Champion-1's idle delegates represent wasted compute.

key_events.summary: Champion-1's f=0.148 (conflating FP8 CUTLASS with all Dense GEMM) was caught by Champion-3 in critique. Champion-1 conceded f=0.0753 in rebuttal, halving the projected E2E from 5.7% to 1.8-2.4%. The debate's adversarial structure caught this error before implementation.

citations.claim: Champion-1 conceded corrected f=0.0753 in rebuttal
citations.quoted_content: **Major concession**: The bottleneck analysis labels "Dense GEMM (FP8 CUTLASS)" with f=0.148, but the Top-15 kernel data shows FP8 CUTLASS total is 7,118 ms = **f=0.0753**.

key_events.summary: Champion-3 in Round 2 discovered that BF16 SSM state (37.1 MB) fits L2 (48 MB) while FP32 (70.6 MB) overflows, enabling a regime change from HBM-bound to L2-resident. The bottleneck analysis conservatively bounded SSM at 1.3x ceiling (correct for same-dtype optimization). The dtype change was a separate optimization class that achieved 3.92x kernel speedup.

citations.claim: OP-003-bf16 discovered regime-shift: BF16 state fits L2, FP32 overflows
citations.quoted_content:
### OP-003-bf16 (Champion-3) -- Score 7.55
- **Strongest empirical evidence**: 3.92x measured under CUDA graph capture
- **Regime-shift insight**: BF16 state (37.1 MB) fits L2 (48 MB), FP32 (70.6 MB) overflows

key_events.summary: Round 1 complete washout (both selections failed) forced Round 2 to explore entirely new approaches. OP-003-bf16 (regime shift) and OP-001-ext (shared expert extension) originated from Round 2 and were the only shipped optimizations.

citations.claim: Round 2 context: Round 1 exhausted, exploring new approaches informed by failures
citations.quoted_content:
## Context
Round 1 exhausted -- CUTLASS split-K blocked by EVT, relu2 fusion marginal (0.58%). Round 2 explores new approaches informed by Round 1 failures.

key_events.summary: Round 2 selected 3 candidates targeting disjoint components (SSM state, FP8 CUTLASS, relu2 chain), enabling additive E2E. Portfolio diversification delivered 2/3 passing, combined 8.11% exceeding individual sums by 0.49% (super-additive from reduced L2 contention).

citations.claim: All 3 Round 2 candidates target different components, enabling additive E2E up to 8.6-11%
citations.quoted_content:
## Combined E2E Potential
If all 3 succeed (different components, additive):
- OP-003-bf16: 5.7% (BS=32)
- OP-002-v2: 1.75-3.4% (BS=32)
- OP-001-ext: 1.1-1.9% (BS=32)
- **Combined: 8.6-11.0%**

key_events.summary: FP8 CUTLASS split-K was attempted in 3 forms across 2 rounds: kGemmSplitKParallel (EVT assertion), EVT bypass (13-17% regression), Triton FP8 split-K (eliminated in debate). Root cause: FP32 workspace + reduced pipeline depth + StreamK already providing redistribution.

citations.claim: Split-K lessons: workspace overhead, pipeline depth, StreamK baseline efficiency
citations.quoted_content:
1. **Split-K parallel is NOT free**: The FP32 workspace write + reduced pipeline depth can exceed the SM utilization benefit
2. **BW models must account for workspace overhead**
3. **StreamK already provides work redistribution**

failure_analysis.root_cause: CUTLASS EVT (GemmUniversalWithVisitor) explicitly disallows kGemmSplitKParallel. Tile-M halving pivot gave only 1.04x.

citations.claim: OP-002-splitk failed: EVT blocks kGemmSplitKParallel, tile-M halving only 1.04x
citations.quoted_content: "OP-002-splitk": {"status": "FAILED", "reason": "EVT blocks kGemmSplitKParallel. Tile-M halving only 1.04x."}

failure_analysis.root_cause: Marlin-only relu2 fusion (46 kernels eliminated) insufficient for BS=32 at 5 iterations. Round 2 added shared expert path (+23 kernels) and 20 iters, passing at 1.31%.

citations.claim: OP-001-relu2 failed at BS=32 avg 0.58%, P50 1.22%, with only 5 iterations

failure_analysis.root_cause: FP32 workspace writes (6x more BW than BF16 direct) + reduced K-loop pipeline efficiency (84%->55%) + StreamK baseline already providing redistribution.

citations.claim: OP-002-v2 regresses 13-17% on all shapes

failure_analysis.kernel_speedup: N/A (70.5 us fused vs ~30 us production = regression)
failure_analysis.root_cause: Single-CTA HBM bandwidth is 44 GB/s on L40S, 13.6x below proposal's 600 GB/s assumption. Even theoretical max (keeping cuBLAS GEMM) gives only 1.47% at BS=32, below 1.50% threshold.

citations.claim: Single-CTA BW 44 GB/s, 13.6x below proposal. Even theoretical max gives 1.47%.
citations.quoted_content:
| Single-CTA HBM BW | 600 GB/s | **44 GB/s** | **13.6x overestimate** |
| Router GEMM time | 2.3 us | **42 us** | 18x slower |

failure_analysis.root_cause: Triton BF16 GEMM cannot beat cuBLAS for K=2688-4096 on L40S. Per-iteration codegen overhead accumulates over 3-4.5x more K-loop iterations than Qwen's K=896.

citations.claim: Triton loses to cuBLAS due to larger K accumulating per-iteration overhead vs Qwen
citations.quoted_content: **Different from Qwen campaign**: The Qwen campaign (op007) showed Triton 1.07x faster for K=896, N=3584. Nemotron has K=2688-4096 (3-4.5x larger K), meaning 3-4.5x more inner-loop iterations.

changelog_entries.entry: OP-003-bf16 discovered a regime shift: BF16 SSM state (37.1 MB) fits L2 (48 MB) while FP32 (70.6 MB) overflows. This changed the kernel from HBM-bound to L2-resident, achieving 3.92x kernel speedup and 6.31% E2E -- exceeding the bottleneck analysis's 1.3x ceiling which was correct for same-dtype optimization. Novel optimization class: dtype change enabling cache-tier transition. Only ~15 LOC across 3 Python/Triton files.

citations.claim: BF16 state enables L2 residency regime shift at BS=32
citations.quoted_content: **Optimization**: Change Mamba SSM state cache from FP32 to BF16, gated by `VLLM_MAMBA_BF16_STATE=1`. At BS=32, FP32 state (70.6 MB) overflows L2 (48 MB) but BF16 state (37.1 MB) fits, enabling a regime shift from HBM-bound to L2-resident.

changelog_entries.entry: FP8 CUTLASS split-K is definitively dead on L40S for M<=32 across ALL tested shapes: 3 attempts across 2 rounds. Root causes: FP32 workspace 6x BW overhead, pipeline depth degradation, StreamK already active. Consistent with Qwen campaign results -- split-K fails across model families on this hardware.

citations.claim: Split-K lessons: workspace overhead, pipeline depth, StreamK baseline efficiency
citations.quoted_content:
1. **Split-K parallel is NOT free**: The FP32 workspace write + reduced pipeline depth can exceed the SM utilization benefit
3. **StreamK already provides work redistribution**

changelog_entries.entry: Triton BF16 GEMM is NOT universally faster than cuBLAS. Qwen: 1.07x at K=896. Nemotron: 0.49-0.81x at K=2688-4096. Crossover is K-dependent: Triton per-iteration codegen overhead accumulates. Only lm_head (K=2688, N=131072) showed Triton competitive at 1.07x where N amortizes overhead.

citations.claim: Triton vs cuBLAS depends on K dimension: 1.07x at K=896, 0.49-0.81x at K=2688-4096
citations.quoted_content: **Different from Qwen campaign**: The Qwen campaign (op007) showed Triton 1.07x faster for K=896, N=3584. Nemotron has K=2688-4096 (3-4.5x larger K), meaning 3-4.5x more inner-loop iterations.

changelog_entries.entry: Per-CTA HBM bandwidth on L40S is ~44-47 GB/s (measured by both Triton and cuBLAS). OP-006 assumed 600 GB/s -- 13.6x overestimate. cuBLAS achieves 187 GB/s for router GEMM by using 4 parallel CTAs. This ceiling makes single-CTA kernel fusion non-viable for BW-bound micro-GEMMs on L40S.

citations.claim: Single-CTA BW on L40S is 44 GB/s, 13.6x below 600 GB/s assumption
citations.quoted_content: | Single-CTA HBM BW | 600 GB/s | **44 GB/s** | **13.6x overestimate** |

changelog_entries.entry: Perfect debate quality score (10/10): 8 proposals across 3 rounds, 100% grounding rate, 100% micro-experiment rate, correct filtering, component diversity. Debate caught Champion-1's f-value error (0.148->0.0753) before implementation, preventing wasted effort on inflated projection.

citations.claim: f-value accuracy critical: debate caught f=0.148 -> f=0.0753 correction
citations.quoted_content: 1. **f-value accuracy is critical**: Champion-1's original f=0.148 included BF16 GEMM. Corrected to f=0.0753 (FP8 CUTLASS only), halving the E2E projection.

changelog_entries.entry: OP-001-ext E2E benefit from kernel ELIMINATION (69 launches removed), not speedup. Triton relu2 execution similar to 2-kernel baseline (data fits L2). Savings: ~0.5 us per eliminated CUDA graph kernel launch x 69. Pattern reliable across batch sizes (2.46% BS=1, 1.31% BS=32).

citations.claim: E2E from kernel elimination, not speedup; Triton fused time similar to 2-kernel baseline
citations.quoted_content: Note: Triton fused kernel execution time is similar to the 2-kernel baseline (data fits L2, so no memory traffic savings). The E2E benefit comes from eliminating CUDA graph kernel launch overhead (~0.5 us per eliminated launch x 23 layers).

changelog_entries.entry: OP-001-relu2 failed at BS=32 avg 0.58% in Round 1 (5 iters, Marlin-only). Round 2 OP-001-ext added shared expert Triton (+23 kernels) and 20 iterations, passing at 1.31%. Two changes: scope expansion and statistical confidence. Round 1 data was noisy with insufficient scope.

citations.claim: Round 1 vs Round 2: scope + statistical confidence drove improvement
citations.quoted_content:
| Kernels eliminated | 46/step | 69/step |
| BS=32 E2E avg | 0.58% (5 iters) | **1.31%** (20 iters) |

changelog_entries.entry: NemotronH hybrid MEMEM* architecture (23 Mamba + 23 MoE + 6 Attention) creates heterogeneous bottlenecks. Third distinct model family profiled (after Qwen dense and GLM hybrid), confirming hybrid architectures expose multiple disjoint optimization surfaces.

citations.claim: NemotronH: 52 layers, Mamba + MoE + Attention, ~3B active per token
citations.quoted_content:
| Architecture | NemotronH (hybrid Mamba + Attention + MoE) |
| Layers | 52 total: 23 Mamba, 23 MoE, 6 Attention |
| Active params/token | ~3B (128 routed experts, top-6, 1 shared) |

changelog_entries.entry: Round 1 bottleneck analysis overcounted FP8 CUTLASS as 14.8% by including prefill-variant kernels. Round 3 re-profiling revealed true decode-only share is ~8.4%. Reduced Amdahl ceiling from 9.4% to 5.3%. The 'Dense GEMM (FP8 CUTLASS)' label also conflated FP8 CUTLASS with BF16 GEMM, contributing to Champion-1's f-value error.

citations.claim: Round 1 overcounted FP8 CUTLASS by including prefill variants; true decode share 8.4% not 14.8%
citations.quoted_content: The Round 1 analysis reported Dense GEMM (FP8 CUTLASS) as 14.8% of decode time. This included larger-M prefill CUTLASS variants (128x128x64, 64x128x128) that are NOT part of the steady-state decode step.

changelog_entries.entry: No transcript monitor active. Fabricated 68.5 us profiling match and f=0.148 conflation would have been intervention candidates. Adversarial debate caught f-value error but 68.5 us fabrication caught only by Champion-3 critique. Evidence that debate partially substitutes for monitoring.

Combined 8.11% vs individual sum 7.62%. 0.49% super-additive gap attributed to reduced L2 contention: BF16 SSM state frees L2 capacity for relu2 epilogue outputs. Within 20-iter noise but consistent with physics.

citations.claim: Combined 8.11% vs individual sum 7.62%, super-additive by 0.49%
